title: Solid geometry word problems
description: Solve problems concerning real-world situations with the volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres.

assessment_items.question_content:
A corn silo in the shape of a cylinder (shown below) was filled at a constant rate. It started empty. After $4$ hours, it held $160\text{ m}^3$ of corn.

**What percent of the silo was filled after $8$ hours?**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest percent.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\%$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cc4ea2e3ecd24111dfc8107ee84ac558c44c4b44)
hints:
### Finding the volume of the silo

First let's find the volume of the silo and the volume of corn in the silo. Then we can divide them to find the percent of the silo that is filled with corn.

$\begin{align}\text{Volume of silo} &= \pi r^2 h \\\\
&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot 10 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot 10 \\\\
&= 160 \pi~~~~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }\pi\\\\
&\approx 502.40 \text{ m}^3\\\\
\end{align}$

### Finding the volume of corn after $8$ hours

We know the silo is filling up at a rate of $40\text{ m}^3$ per hour because after $4$ hours, there are $160\text{ m}^3$ of corn in the silo.

After $8$ hours there will be $40 \cdot 8 = 320 \text{ m}^3$ of corn in the silo.

### Calculating the final answer

To find the the percent of the silo that's filled after $8$ hours, we divide the volume of the corn by the volume of the silo.

$\dfrac{\text{Volume of corn in  silo}}{\text{Volume of silo}} = \dfrac{320 \text{ m}^3}{502.40\text{ m}^3} \approx 0.64 \approx 64\%$

### The answer

The silo is about $64\%$ filled with corn.

assessment_items.question_content:
An empty cup in the shape of a cylinder (pictured below) is being filled with water.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18faa006f9d288cf2130303caa021619f2ee4027)

The cup is filled at a constant rate. The table below shows the amount of water in the cup at specific times.

Number of seconds | Amount of water $(\text{cm}^3)$ 
:-: | :-: 
$0$ | $0$ 
$3$ | $75$ 
$6$ | $150$ 

**Find the percent of the cup that is filled with water after $7$ seconds.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest percent.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\%$
hints: First let's find the volume of the cup and the volume of water in the cup. Then we can divide them to find the percent of the cup that is filled with water.

$\begin{align}\text{Volume of cup} &= \pi r^2 h\\\\
&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot 7 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot 7 \\\\
&=63 \pi~~~~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }\pi\\\\
&=197.82 \text{ cm}^3\\\\
\end{align}$
We know the cup is filling up at a rate of $25\text{ cm}^3$ per second because after $3$ seconds, there are $75\text{ cm}^3$ of water in the cup.
After $7$ seconds there will be $25 \cdot 7 = 175 \text{ cm}^3$ of water in the cup.

$\begin{align} \text{Percent of the cup filled}&=\dfrac{\text{Volume of water in cup}}{\text{Volume of cup}}\\\\
 &= \dfrac{175 \text{ cm}^3}{197.82\text{ cm}^3}\\\\ \\
&\approx88\%\\\\
\end{align}$
After $7$ seconds the cup is about $88\%$ filled with water.

assessment_items.question_content:
George has a candle in the shape of a cylinder.

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d295f326ffe5970a02d029b66f489bab330be30b)

The candle melts at a constant rate. The table below shows the amount of candle, in cubic centimeters, melted off at the given number of hours after burning started.

Number of hours | Amount of candle melted off $(\text{cm}^3)$ 
:-: | :-: 
$2$ | $60$ 
$4$ | $120$ 
$6$ | $180$ 

**Find the percent of the candle that is melted off after $7$ hours.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest percent.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\%$
hints: First let's find the volume of the candle and the volume of wax melted off. Then we can divide them to find the percent of the candle that is melted off.

$\begin{align}\text{Volume of candle} &= \pi r^2 h\\\\
&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot 6 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot 6 \\\\
&=96 \pi~~~~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }\pi\\\\
&=301.44 \text{ cm}^3\\\\
\end{align}$
We know the candle is burning at a rate of $30\text{ cm}^3$ per hour because after $2$ hours, $60\text{ cm}^3$ of the candle was melted off.
After $7$ hours there will be $30 \cdot 7 = 210 \text{ cm}^3$ of candle melted off.

$\begin{align} \text{Percent candle melted off}&=\dfrac{\text{Amount candle melted off}}{\text{Original volume of candle}}\\\\
 &= \dfrac{210 \text{ cm}^3}{301.44\text{ cm}^3}\\\\ \\
&\approx70\%\\\\
\end{align}$
After $7$ hours about $70\%$ of the candle is melted off.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](     web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/01b810be59a38c75076b9bd25e5395d04e3ac1fd )

The cup was filled at a constant rate. It started empty. After $2$ seconds, it held $70\text{ cm}^3$ of water.

**Find the percent of the cup that is filled with water after $3$ seconds.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest percent.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\%$
hints:
### Overview

We have the dimensions of the cup, so we can find its volume.

We also have enough information to find the rate at which the cup filled with water. Once we find the rate, we can find the volume of water in the cup after $3$ seconds.

At that point, we'll know the volume of the cup and the volume of the water in the cup. We can divide these quantities to find what fraction of the cup is filled with water.

### Step 1: Find the volume of the cone

$\begin{align}\text{Volume of original cone} &=  \pi r^2\dfrac{h}3\\\\
&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot\dfrac73 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot\dfrac73 \\\\
&= \dfrac{112}{3} \pi~~~~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }\pi\\\\\\\\
&=117.23 \text{ cm}^3\\\\
\end{align}$

### Step 2: Find the rate at which the cone filled with water

We know the cup is filling up at a rate of $35\text{ cm}^3$ per second because after $2$ seconds, there were $\text{70 cm}^3$ of water in the cup.

> $\dfrac{70 \text{ cm}^3}{2\text{ seconds}} = 35\text{ cm}^3\text{ per second}$

### Step 3: Find the volume of water in the cone after $3$ seconds

After $3$ seconds there will be $35 \cdot 3 = 105 \text{ cm}^3$ of water in the cup.

### Step 4: Find fraction of the cup that's filled with water

$\begin{align} \text{Fraction of cup filled}&=\dfrac{\text{Volume of water in cup}}{\text{Volume of cup}}\\\\
 &= \dfrac{105 \text{ cm}^3}{117.23\text{ cm}^3}\\\\ \\
&\approx 0.90 ~~~~\gray{\text{which can be written as }90\%}\\\\
\end{align}$

### The answer

After $3$ seconds, the cup is about $90\%$ filled with water.

assessment_items.question_content:
$4$ days ago, Emily blew up a balloon (that is a perfect sphere) to a radius of $5\text{ cm}$. Since then, the balloon has lost air and decreased by $0.5\text{ cm}$ each day. 

**What is the ratio of the current volume of the balloon to the volume of the balloon $4$ days ago?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First let's find the original volume of the balloon and the current volume of the balloon. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

First, let's find the original volume of the balloon.

$\begin{align}\text{Original volume of balloon} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot5^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43 \cdot \pi \cdot125 \\\\
&= \dfrac{500}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$
The balloon has decreased in radius by $4 \cdot 0.5 = 2 \text{ cm}$. So, the current radius of the balloon is $\blueD3\text{ cm}$.

Next, let's find the current volume of the balloon.

$\begin{align}\text{Current volume of balloon} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot\blueD3^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43 \cdot\pi \cdot27 \\\\
&= \dfrac{108}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

Now, let's divide the current volume of the balloon by the original volume of the balloon to get the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Current volume of balloon}}{\text{Original volume of balloon}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{ \dfrac{108\pi}{3}}{\dfrac{500 \pi}{3}}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{108\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac{3}{500 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{108\pi}}{\cancel{3}} \cdot\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{{500 \pi}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{108\cancel{\pi}}}{{500 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{108}{500}\\\\
&= \dfrac{27}{125}
\end{align}$
The ratio of the current volume of the balloon to the original volume of the balloon is $\dfrac{27}{125}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Manuel bought a balloon (that is a perfect sphere) with a radius of $2 \text{ cm}$. He wanted his balloon to be bigger, so he blew $2$ big breaths of air into the balloon. Each big breath increased the balloon's radius by $1 \text{ cm}$.

**What is the ratio of the current volume of the balloon to the original volume of the balloon? **  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First let's find the original volume of the balloon and the current volume of the balloon. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align}\text{Original volume of balloon} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot2^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot8 \\\\
&= \dfrac{32}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$
We know the current radius of the balloon is $\blueD4 \text{ cm}$ because Manuel blew in $2$ breaths of air and each increased the radius by $1\text{ cm}$.

$\begin{align}\text{Current volume of balloon} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot\blueD4^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43 \cdot\pi \cdot64 \\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Current volume of balloon}}{\text{Original volume of balloon}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{ \dfrac{256\pi}{3}}{\dfrac{32 \pi}{3}}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{256\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac{3}{32 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{256\pi}}{\cancel{3}} \cdot\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{{32 \pi}} \\\
&= \dfrac{{256\cancel{\pi}}}{{32 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\
&= \dfrac{256}{32}\\\\
&=8\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the current volume of the balloon to the original volume of the balloon is $8$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Andy had a snowball (a perfect sphere) with a radius of $3 \text{ cm}$. He wanted the snowball to be bigger, so he spent $4$ seconds packing more snow onto it. Each second he spent packing, the snowball's radius increased by $0.25 \text{ cm}$.

**What is the ratio of the current volume of the snowball to the original volume of the snowball? **  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First let's find the original volume of the snowball and the current volume of the snowball. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align}\text{Original volume of snowball} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot3^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot27 \\\\
&= 36 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$
We know the current radius of the snowball is $\blueD4 \text{ cm}$ because Andy spent $4$ seconds packing on snow and each second increased the radius by $0.25\text{ cm}$.

$\begin{align}\text{Current volume of snowball} &=  \dfrac43 \pi r^3\\\\
&=\dfrac43\cdot\pi\cdot\blueD4^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot64\\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Current volume of snowball}}{\text{Original volume of snowball}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{256 \pi}{3}}{36\pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{256\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac{1}{36 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{256\pi}}{108\pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{256\cancel{\pi}}}{{108 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{108}\\\\
&= \dfrac{64}{27}
\end{align}$
The ratio of the current volume of the snowball to the original volume of the snowball is $\dfrac{64}{27}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f903f91f3d80c0a2c658d0069123d4aa6b0172a1) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/455b23a19c49a049b87860b4c4ddcad095bb1cba)

The cone and the cylinder have the same base and the same height.

**What is the ratio of the volume of the cone to the volume of the cylinder?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of the cone and the volume of the cylinder. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of the cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3\\\\&= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot\dfrac53 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot\dfrac53 \\\\
&= \dfrac{20}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of the cylinder} &= \pi r^2 h \\\\&= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot5 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot5 \\\\
&= 20 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of the cone}}{\text{Volume of the cylinder}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{20}{3} \pi}{20 \pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{20\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac1{20 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{\cancel{20\pi}}{3} \cdot\dfrac1{\cancel{20 \pi}} \\\\\
&= \dfrac13\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of the cone to the volume of the cylinder is $\dfrac13$. [[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ee03d6d72b1de121718d407ad25318369a39cfb5) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a2a02b811cbd072072cf8b86c6c2871117f42355)

**What is the ratio of the volume of the cone to the volume of the cylinder?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of the cone and the volume of the cylinder. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of the cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3 \\\\&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot\dfrac53 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot\dfrac53 \\\\
&=15 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of the cylinder} &= \pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot5 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot4 \\\\
&= 16 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of the cone}}{\text{Volume of the cylinder}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{15 \pi}{16 \pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{15\cancel{ \pi}}{16 \cancel{\pi}}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{15}{16}\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of the cone to the volume of the cylinder is $\dfrac{15}{16}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f89d5340364fc2bac4c44a6b843cdea0f3842b4) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/06de2c6e305786734a7edf573d8fb2e7af2b1063)

**What is the ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each cylinder. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder A} &=\pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot3 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot3 \\\\
&= 27 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder B}&=\pi r^2 h\\\\ &= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot4 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot4 \\\\
&= 16 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Cylinder A}}{\text{Volume of Cylinder B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{27 \pi}{16 \pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{27\cancel{\pi}}{16\cancel{\pi}} \\\\\
&= \dfrac{27}{16}\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B is $\dfrac{27}{16}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4e24899a4011912e90c976c48b10c4e15d6fc2bd) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ca4c22f67e8f53d941b1562cf5f36eefee14ebf5)

**What is the ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each cylinder. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder A} &=\pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot4 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot4 \\\\
&= 36 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder B} &=\pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot5^2 \cdot2 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot2 \\\\
&= 50 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Cylinder A}}{\text{Volume of Cylinder B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{36 \pi}{50 \pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{36\cancel{\pi}}{50\cancel{\pi}} \\\\\
&= \dfrac{36}{50}\\\\
&= \dfrac{18}{25}\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B is $\dfrac{18}{25}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4e24899a4011912e90c976c48b10c4e15d6fc2bd) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9324d249169280001ce81fba1046218d9ccb9850)

**What is the ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each cylinder. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder A} &=\pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot4 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot4 \\\\
&= 36 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Cylinder B} &=\pi r^2 h\\\\&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot3 \\\\
&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot3 \\\\
&= 48 \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Cylinder A}}{\text{Volume of Cylinder B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{36 \pi}{48 \pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{36\cancel{\pi}}{48\cancel{\pi}} \\\\
&= \dfrac{36}{48}\\\\
&= \dfrac{3}{4}
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Cylinder A to the volume of Cylinder B is $\dfrac34$.

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3d8c83a5afba948d40f2c8390aa9947d696410b4)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/29462f51e68ed3497976f63b0e5a9010e733b911)  

**What is the ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each sphere. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere A} &=\dfrac 43\pi r^3 \\\\&=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot2^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot8\\\\
&= \dfrac{32}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere B} &=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot4^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot64\\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Sphere A}}{\text{Volume of Sphere B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{ \dfrac{32\pi}{3}}{\dfrac{256 \pi}{3}}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{32\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac{3}{256 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{32\pi}}{\cancel{3}} \cdot\dfrac{\cancel{3}}{{256 \pi}} \\\
&= \dfrac{{32\cancel{\pi}}}{{256 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\
&= \dfrac{32}{256}\\\
&= \dfrac18\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B is $\dfrac{1}{8}$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/49b69ba047b2f2967ca91859e3912bf9faee28c8)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8898b4a9bf2465b6980e02a75349fde26ec0a00c)  

**What is the ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each sphere. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere A} &=\dfrac 43\pi r^3\\\\&=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot3^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot27\\\\
&= \dfrac{108}{3} \pi\\\\
&=36\pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere B} &=\dfrac 43\pi r^3\\\\&=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot1^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot1\\\\
&= \dfrac{4}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Sphere A}}{\text{Volume of Sphere B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{ 36\pi}{\dfrac{4 \pi}{3}}  \\\\
&=36\pi\cdot\dfrac{3}{4 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{108\pi}}{4\pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{108\cancel{\pi}}}{{4 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\
&= \dfrac{108}{4}\\\\
&= 27\\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B is $27$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/aa228014ef7a793f546e14d54352598b1d562a74)
![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e343ca587b540320189a7100c364dc0d54334650)  

**What is the ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's find the volume of each sphere. Then we can divide them to find the ratio.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere A} &=\dfrac 43\pi r^3\\\\&=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot4^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot64\\\\
&= \dfrac{256}{3} \pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of Sphere B} &=\dfrac 43\pi r^3\\\\&=  \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot3^3 \\\\
&= \dfrac43\cdot \pi \cdot27\\\\
&= \dfrac{108}{3} \pi\\\\
&=36\pi\\\\
\end{align}$

$\begin{align} \text{Volume ratio} &= \dfrac{\text{Volume of Sphere A}}{\text{Volume of Sphere B}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\dfrac{256 \pi}{3}}{36\pi}  \\\\
&= \dfrac{256\pi}{3} \cdot\dfrac{1}{36 \pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{256\pi}}{108\pi} \\\\
&= \dfrac{{256\cancel{\pi}}}{{108 \cancel{\pi}}} \\\
&= \dfrac{256}{108}\\\
&= \dfrac{64}{27}\\\
\end{align}$
The ratio of the volume of Sphere A to the volume of Sphere B is $\dfrac{64}{27}$.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A cone with radius $3$ units is shown below. Its volume is $57$ cubic units.

![](   web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/0b369268f562cf200fd95b7239419cc2bb6aec47    )



**Find the height of the cone.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cone.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3\\\\
57&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
57&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
57&= 3 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{57}{\blueD{3 \pi}}&= \dfrac{3 \pi h}{\blueD{3 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 3 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{57}{\blueD{3 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{3 \pi}h}{\cancel{\blueD{3 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{57}{\blueD{3 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx6.05
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 6.05$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cone with radius $2$ units is shown below. Its volume is $29$ cubic units.

![](  web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/19bb9a1c1378e1027766a06619931e6f582b7a92    )


**Find the height of the cone.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cone.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3\\\\
29&= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
29&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
29\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot\dfrac h3 \cdot\purpleD3~~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by } {\purpleD{ 3}}\\\\
29\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot\dfrac h{\cancel3} \cdot\cancel{\purpleD3}\\\\
87&= 4 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{87}{\blueD{4\pi}}&= \dfrac{4\pi h}{\blueD{4\pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ {4\pi}}}\\\\
\dfrac{87}{\blueD{4\pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{4\pi}h}{\cancel{\blueD{{4\pi}}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{87}{\blueD{4\pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\\
h &\approx6.93
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 6.93$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cone with radius $5$ units is shown below. Its volume is $105$ cubic units.

![](   web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/8553f27abcb3491e2aa6064270e55aac202723fb    )


**Find the height of the cone.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cone.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3\\\\
105&= \pi \cdot5^2 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
105&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
105\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot\dfrac h3 \cdot\purpleD3~~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by } {\purpleD{ 3}}\\\\
105\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot\dfrac h{\cancel3} \cdot\cancel{\purpleD3}\\\\
315&= 25 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{315}{\blueD{25 \pi}}&= \dfrac{25 \pi h}{\blueD{25 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 25 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{315}{\blueD{25 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{25 \pi}h}{\cancel{\blueD{25 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{315}{\blueD{25 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx4.01
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 4.01$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cone with radius $4$ units is shown below. Its volume is $50$ cubic units.

![](   web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/d0f98c9e62986b2e27d8adea903967c030e7eac2  )


**Find the height of the cone.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cone.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cone} &= \pi r^2 \dfrac{h}3\\\\
50&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
50&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot\dfrac h3 \\\\
50\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot\dfrac h3 \cdot\purpleD3~~~~~~~\text{multiply both sides by } {\purpleD{ 3}}\\\\
50\cdot\purpleD3&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot\dfrac h{\cancel3} \cdot\cancel{\purpleD3}\\\\
150&= 16 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{150}{\blueD{16\pi}}&= \dfrac{16\pi h}{\blueD{16\pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ {16\pi}}}\\\\
\dfrac{150}{\blueD{16\pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{16\pi}h}{\cancel{\blueD{{16\pi}}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{150}{\blueD{16\pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\\
h &\approx2.99
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 2.99$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cylinder with radius $3$ units is shown below. Its volume is $86$ cubic units.

![](  web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/7cb2e60acdd4ed47f3a8aa23c7f40ce4c2858fdc    )



**Find the height of the cylinder.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cylinder.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cylinder} &= \pi r^2 {h}\\\\
86&= \pi \cdot3^2 \cdot h \\\\
86&= \pi \cdot 9 \cdot h \\\\
86&= 9 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{86}{\blueD{9 \pi}}&= \dfrac{9 \pi h}{\blueD{9 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 9 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{86}{\blueD{9 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{9 \pi}h}{\blueD{\cancel{9 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{86}{\blueD{9 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx3.04
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 3.04$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cylinder with radius $2$ units is shown below. Its volume is $75$ cubic units.

![](  web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/e7fbbc8f80b784c5ca58ad1e4a63581543b01061  )



**Find the height of the cylinder.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cylinder.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cylinder} &= \pi r^2 {h}\\\\
75&= \pi \cdot2^2 \cdot h \\\\
75&= \pi \cdot 4 \cdot h \\\\
75&= 4 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{75}{\blueD{4 \pi}}&= \dfrac{4 \pi h}{\blueD{4 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 4 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{75}{\blueD{4 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{4 \pi}h}{\blueD{\cancel{4 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{75}{\blueD{4 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx5.97
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 5.97$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cylinder with radius $4$ units is shown below. Its volume is $201$ cubic units.

![](  web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/c9d3df74c38748ac705398b13d34c1502a82c0da   )



**Find the height of the cylinder.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cylinder.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cylinder} &= \pi r^2 {h}\\\\
201&= \pi \cdot4^2 \cdot h \\\\
201&= \pi \cdot 16 \cdot h \\\\
201&= 16 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{201}{\blueD{16 \pi}}&= \dfrac{16 \pi h}{\blueD{16 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 16 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{201}{\blueD{16 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{16 \pi}h}{\blueD{\cancel{16 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{201}{\blueD{16 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx4
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 4$ units

assessment_items.question_content:
A cylinder with radius $5$ units is shown below. Its volume is $157$ cubic units.

![](  web+graphie://cdn.kastatic.org/ka-perseus-graphie/8d496a22cdb6017fa68d1b98cc6f4be05c7189f9    )



**Find the height of the cylinder.**  
*Use $3.14$ for $\pi$ and round your final answer to the nearest hundredth.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints: Let's set up an equation using the formula for the volume of a cylinder.

$\begin{align} \text{Volume of a cylinder} &= \pi r^2 {h}\\\\
157&= \pi \cdot5^2 \cdot h \\\\
157&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot h \\\\
157&= 25 \pi h\\\\
\dfrac{157}{\blueD{25 \pi}}&= \dfrac{25 \pi h}{\blueD{25 \pi}} ~~~~~~~\text{divide both sides by } {\blueD{ 25 \pi}}\\\\
\dfrac{157}{\blueD{25 \pi}}&= \dfrac{\cancel{25 \pi}h}{\blueD{\cancel{25 \pi}}}\\\\
h &=\dfrac{157}{\blueD{25 \pi}}~~~~~~~~~~\text{use 3.14 for }
\pi\\\\
h &\approx2
\end{align}$

The answer:

>$h \approx 2$ units
